serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of ikan bilis fried rice as typically served in a Singapore hawker centre or café, approximately 300 g.

nutrient_source.title: Nasi Goreng Ikan Bilis (Fried Rice with Dried Anchovies) — HPB Singapore Foodo / Energy and Nutrient Composition of Food
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Foodo lists Nasi Goreng Ikan Bilis as a standard hawker dish. Per-100 g values are derived from the HPB Energy and Nutrient Composition of Food database entry for fried rice with ikan bilis. Minor adaptation was made to ensure the values are representative of a standard preparation that may include additional chilli and vegetables, consistent with typical hawker versions. Values are representative of the standard preparation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — White rice, fried rice analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for ikan bilis fried rice. GI of 64 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for white rice fried rice (GI ~64, medium category), which is the closest analogous preparation. The addition of ikan bilis (dried anchovies), egg, and vegetables does not substantially alter the GI relative to the rice base. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (64 × 73.5) / 100 ≈ 47.0, based on 300 g serving containing approximately 73.5 g carbohydrate.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from dried anchovies and soy sauce
hypertension.guidance: Ikan bilis fried rice contains approximately 420 mg sodium per 100 g (approximately 1260 mg per standard 300 g serving), which represents more than half the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg for adults with hypertension; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size or request reduced soy sauce and ikan bilis.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic load from refined white rice base
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a glycemic index of approximately 64 (medium) and a glycemic load of approximately 47 per standard 300 g serving, ikan bilis fried rice can cause significant postprandial blood glucose elevation; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to 150–200 g, pair with additional non-starchy vegetables, and monitor blood glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate fat content; ikan bilis provides beneficial omega-3 fatty acids
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The high sodium content (approximately 1260 mg per serving) is a concern for cardiovascular disease risk; however, ikan bilis (dried anchovies) contributes small amounts of omega-3 fatty acids and calcium, and the overall saturated fat content is moderate at 1.1 g per 100 g, so individuals with cardiovascular disease should focus on sodium reduction and portion control.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Ikan Bilis Fried Rice (Nasi Goreng Ikan Bilis; 江鱼仔炒饭) is a popular Singaporean hawker dish consisting of wok-fried cooked white rice stir-fried with crispy dried anchovies (ikan bilis), egg, garlic, shallots, chilli, and mixed vegetables, seasoned with soy sauce and fish sauce. The dish is characterised by a savoury, slightly salty, and mildly spicy flavour profile, with the ikan bilis providing a crunchy texture and umami depth. It is commonly served as a one-dish meal at hawker centres, coffee shops (kopitiams), and casual cafés across Singapore, and is closely related to the Indonesian and Malaysian nasi goreng tradition. Nutritionally, ikan bilis fried rice provides approximately 168 kcal per 100 g, with a moderate protein content of 5.8 g per 100 g contributed by the anchovies and egg, and a carbohydrate content of 24.5 g per 100 g from the white rice base. The dish is high in sodium (approximately 420 mg per 100 g) due to the dried anchovies and soy sauce, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated at 64 (medium), and the glycemic load per standard 300 g serving is approximately 47, which is clinically significant for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. Ikan bilis are a traditional Southeast Asian ingredient rich in calcium and small amounts of omega-3 fatty acids. The dish is generally halal-certified in Singapore hawker settings.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Nasi Goreng Ikan Bilis, which is the closest approved primary source for this Singaporean hawker dish. The GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for white rice fried rice, as no direct GI measurement for ikan bilis fried rice was found. Confidence is medium rather than high because the nutrient values are adapted rather than an exact match to this specific field survey restaurant preparation.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Singapore Foodo (Nasi Goreng Ikan Bilis) and are representative of the standard hawker preparation. The field survey listing from Lucky Cafe - North Canal Road may differ in exact ingredient proportions. 2. GI of 64 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for white rice fried rice; no direct GI measurement for ikan bilis fried rice was located in approved sources. 3. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source database for this dish. 4. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source database for this dish. 5. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source database for this dish. 6. trans_fat_g is 0.0; confirmed negligible/trace per HPB source for this preparation. 7. The cuisine tag in the input JSON lists 'Filipino'; however, ikan bilis fried rice is a Singaporean hawker dish with Malay/Indonesian culinary roots and is widely served across Singapore. The cuisine has been corrected to 'Singaporean'. 8. The input food_name 'Ikanbilis Fried Rice' has been normalised to the canonical English form 'Ikan Bilis Fried Rice'. 9. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (64 × 73.5 g carbohydrate in 300 g serving) / 100 ≈ 47.0.
